preamble: The shift from a voiced dental fricative [ð] to a rhotic [r] or tap [ɾ] is a form of rhotacism, typically occurring in intervocalic positions where the fricative weakens into a resonant sound.

examples.note: In rapid or informal speech in some Caribbean and Southern Spanish dialects, the intervocalic dental fricative (a reflex of /d/) can be perceived as or merge with the alveolar tap [ɾ].
Common in the Bressan dialect where historical dental sounds shifted toward rhotic realizations.
Occurs in some dialects where the dental fricative is realized as a tap [ɾ] or rhotic [r].

sources: Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
